 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-7q1","title":"Wrap all phase functions with try_step calls","description":"# Task: Wrap all phase functions with try_step calls

## Context
Part of EPIC: Per-Phase Error Reporting (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-fkf)

## What to Do
Update all install_phase_* functions to use try_step() for each operation:

### Before
```bash
install_phase_languages() {
    curl -fsSL https://bun.sh/install | bash
    curl -fsSL https://sh.rustup.rs | sh -s -- -y
    # ...
}
```

### After
```bash
install_phase_languages() {
    CURRENT_PHASE_NAME="Language Runtimes"

    if ! command -v bun &>/dev/null; then
        try_step "Installing Bun runtime" \
            "acfs_curl https://bun.sh/install | bash" || return 1
    fi

    if ! command -v cargo &>/dev/null; then
        try_step "Installing Rust via rustup" \
            "acfs_curl https://sh.rustup.rs | sh -s -- -y" || return 1
    fi
    # ...
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- All phases use try_step for each operation
- Each step has descriptive name
- Existing command skip logic preserved
- return 1 on any step failure

## Files to Modify
- install.sh: All install_phase_* functions","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T12:44:02.642601-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T15:02:12.698982-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T15:02:12.698982-05:00","close_reason":"Wrapped all 10 phase functions with set_phase() and try_step() calls. -{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-8h4","title":"Onboard TUI Auth Flow Integration","description":"## Purpose

Add guided authentication flow to the `onboard` TUI that runs after ACFS installation.
This helps users authenticate all their services in a structured, step-by-step manner.

## Context

The onboard TUI (`packages/onboard/`) is an interactive tutorial that teaches users
Linux basics and the agent workflow. This epic extends it with an "auth" module that
guides users through authenticating each installed service.

## User Flow

After ACFS installs, user runs `onboard`:

```
$ onboard

Welcome to ACFS! What would you like to learn?

  [1] Linux Basics
  [2] Terminal Navigation  
  [3] Git Fundamentals
  [4] Authenticate Services  ← NEW
  [5] Your First Agent Session
```

Selecting "Authenticate Services":

```
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  Service Authentication
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Let's connect your services. We'll go through each one.

  [ ] Tailscale     - Secure VPS access
  [✓] Claude Code   - Already authenticated!
  [ ] Codex CLI     - OpenAI coding agent
  [ ] Gemini CLI    - Google coding agent

Press Enter to start with Tailscale...
```

Then for each service:

```
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  Tailscale Setup
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Tailscale gives you secure remote access to your VPS.
Once connected, you can SSH from anywhere!

Run this command:
  
  sudo tailscale up

This will open a browser (or show a URL).
Log in with your Google account.

[Press Enter when done, or 's' to skip]
```

After auth completes:

```
✓ Tailscale connected!
  Your Tailscale IP: 100.64.x.y
  
  Now you can SSH to this machine from any device
  with Tailscale installed:
  
  ssh user@your-vps.tailnet-name.ts.net

[Press Enter to continue to next service]
```

## Technical Implementation

### Auth Module Structure

```
packages/onboard/
├── src/
│   ├── lessons/
│   │   ├── auth/
│   │   │   ├── index.ts      # Auth lesson entry
│   │   │   ├── tailscale.ts  # Tailscale auth flow
│   │   │   ├── claude.ts     # Claude auth flow
│   │   │   ├── codex.ts      # Codex auth flow
│   │   │   └── gemini.ts     # Gemini auth flow
```

### Service Auth Check Functions

```typescript
// Check if service is authenticated
async function checkTailscaleAuth(): Promise<boolean> {
  const result = await exec('tailscale status --json');
  const status = JSON.parse(result.stdout);
  return status.BackendState === 'Running';
}

async function checkClaudeAuth(): Promise<boolean> {
  // Check for config file
  const configPath = path.join(os.homedir(), '.claude', 'config.json');
  return fs.existsSync(configPath);
}

// etc.
```

### Interactive Flow

Each service follows the pattern:
1. Check if already authenticated
2. If yes, show ✓ and skip
3. If no, explain service and show auth command
4. Wait for user to run command
5. Verify auth succeeded
6. Show success message and tips
7. Move to next service

## Dependencies

- Service catalog (same as web wizard)
- Auth check functions for each service
- Terminal UI framework (blessed, ink, or similar)

## Acceptance Criteria

1. `onboard` has "Authenticate Services" option
2. Each installed service has an auth flow
3. Auth status is checked before and after
4. Clear instructions and visual feedback
5. User can skip individual services
6. Progress is tracked (which services authed)","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-21T17:01:46.476515-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T17:01:46.476515-05:00"}

 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-8mv","title":"Add --strict flag for legacy checksum behavior","description":"# Task: Add --strict flag for legacy checksum behavior

## Context
Part of EPIC: Checkpoint-Based Checksum Recovery (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-tx7)

## What to Do
Add --strict flag that restores the original behavior where ANY checksum mismatch aborts installation.

### Behavior
- Without --strict (default): Use new recovery flow
- With --strict: All tools treated as critical, any mismatch aborts

### Use Cases
- Security-conscious users who want no exceptions
- CI/CD environments where reproducibility matters
- Auditing/compliance scenarios

## Acceptance Criteria
- --strict flag parsed in argument handling
- When set, all tools treated as critical
- Help text documents the flag
- Default behavior is recovery flow

## Files to Modify
- install.sh: Argument parsing, pass flag to security functions","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T12:44:39.830135-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T15:04:39.956587-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T15:04:39.956587-05:00","close_reason":"Added --strict flag to install.sh that sets ACFS_STRICT_MODE=true. When enabled, all tools are treated as CRITICAL and any checksum mismatch aborts installation. Updated header documentation.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-8mv","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-v8a","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T12:47:33.579339-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

1. Test on fresh ACFS install (all tools present)
2. Test on partial install (some tools missing)
3. Test each category individually
4. Test --dry-run mode
5. Test --yes mode (non-interactive)
6. Test failure handling (simulate failures)
7. Test logging output

- Fresh VPS with full ACFS install
- Existing VPS with previous ACFS version
- Missing tools (should skip gracefully)
- Network failures (should report and continue)
- Permission issues (should report and continue)

## Considerations
- Need a test VPS or Docker container
- Some tests may need mocking (network failures)
- Should test as both root and non-root user

- [ ] Dry-run shows accurate preview
- [ ] Failures handled gracefully
- [ ] Logs are comprehensive and useful
- [ ] No regressions in existing functionality","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T13:27:10.04603-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T15:38:12.190216-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T15:38:12.190216-05:00","close_reason":"Created comprehensive Docker-based E2E test (tests/vm/test_acfs_update.sh) covering: --help, --dry-run, --quiet, --yes modes; category filters (--agents-only, --shell-only, --no-apt); log file creation; exit codes; missing tool handling; version display. ### Cloud CLI Tests

- 1: Fail (with fix suggestion)
- 2: Skip (not installed)
- 3: Info (not configured, but optional)

- PostgreSQL connection and role verified
- Status codes used consistently
- Timeout on external calls (prevent hangs)

 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-bfv","title":"Add tailscale module to acfs.manifest.yaml","description":"## Task

Add Tailscale as a module in the ACFS manifest.

## Module Definition

```yaml
tailscale:
  name: Tailscale
  description: Zero-config mesh VPN for secure remote access
  category: networking  # New category or under 'tools'
  phase: cli_tools  # Install early, before agents
  enabled_by_default: true
  
  install:
    method: script
    script: |
      # Add Tailscale apt repository
      curl -fsSL https://pkgs.tailscale.com/stable/ubuntu/$(lsb_release -cs).noarmor.gpg \
        | sudo tee /usr/share/keyrings/tailscale-archive-keyring.gpg >/dev/null
      echo "deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/tailscale-archive-keyring.gpg] \
        https://pkgs.tailscale.com/stable/ubuntu $(lsb_release -cs) main" \
        | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/tailscale.list
      sudo apt-get update
      sudo apt-get install -y tailscale
      sudo systemctl enable tailscaled
  
  verify:
    command: tailscale version
    expected_pattern: "^tailscale"
  
  post_install_message: |
    Tailscale installed! To connect your VPS to your Tailscale network:
      sudo tailscale up
    Then log in with your Google account at the URL shown.
  
  docs_url: https://tailscale.com/kb/
  
  tags:
    - networking
    - vpn
    - security
    - google-sso
```

## Considerations

1. **Ubuntu version handling**: Use `$(lsb_release -cs)` for codename
2. **Fallback for older Ubuntu**: Tailscale supports back to Ubuntu 18.04
3. **Systemd enablement**: Ensure tailscaled starts on boot
4. **Skip if already installed**: Check before re-installing

## Verification

After this task:
- `acfs --list-modules` shows tailscale
- `acfs --print-plan` includes tailscale in cli_tools phase","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:57:40.857933-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T17:32:04.587508-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T17:32:04.587508-05:00","close_reason":"Added network.tailscale module to manifest. Generator creates install_network.sh. Module is Phase 5, enabled by default, uses apt repository method."}
 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-bq3","title":"Ubuntu Auto-Upgrade Feature","description":"# Ubuntu Auto-Upgrade Feature

## Overview
Automatically detect Ubuntu version and upgrade to the latest stable release (25.10) before running the main ACFS installation. The entire process should be **fully automated and non-interactive**.

## Problem Statement
Many cloud providers (OVH, Contabo, Hetzner) default to older Ubuntu versions (22.04, 24.04). ACFS works best on the latest Ubuntu, and some packages may not be available on older versions. Currently, ACFS just warns about old versions but doesn't help upgrade.

## Solution
Add a pre-installation phase that:
1. Detects current Ubuntu version
2. Calculates upgrade path to target version
3. Automatically performs sequential do-release-upgrade operations
4. Handles reboots with automatic resume via systemd service
5. Continues ACFS installation after reaching target version

## Design Philosophy
- **Fully automated**: No user interaction required once started
- **Resilient**: Handle reboots, network issues, and failures gracefully
- **Observable**: Comprehensive logging for debugging
- **Opt-out available**: Users can skip with --skip-ubuntu-upgrade

## Default Behavior
With the standard one-liner:
```bash
curl -fsSL .../install.sh | bash -s -- --yes --mode vibe
```
The installer will:
1. Detect Ubuntu version
2. If below 25.10, automatically upgrade (no prompts)
3. Reboot as needed
4. Resume and continue upgrading until at target
5. Proceed with ACFS installation

## Opt-out
```bash
curl -fsSL .../install.sh | bash -s -- --yes --mode vibe --skip-ubuntu-upgrade
```

## Technical Constraints
1. Ubuntu only allows upgrading to NEXT version (no skipping)
   - 22.04 → 24.04 (LTS-to-LTS is special case)
   - 24.04 → 24.10 → 25.04 → 25.10 (sequential)
2. Each upgrade requires a reboot
3. do-release-upgrade takes 30-60 minutes per version
4. SSH sessions will disconnect during reboot (users must reconnect)
5. Need systemd service for automatic resume after reboot

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Detects Ubuntu version accurately
- [ ] Calculates correct upgrade path
- [ ] Performs upgrade non-interactively
- [ ] Creates systemd service for post-reboot resume
- [ ] Chains multiple upgrades without manual intervention
- [ ] Resumes ACFS installation after final upgrade
- [ ] Cleans up systemd service after completion
- [ ] Handles errors with clear logging
- [ ] Works across SSH disconnection/reconnection","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:18:52.892435-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T17:17:59.471452-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T17:17:59.471452-05:00","close_reason":"All sub-tasks complete: ubu.1-ubu.12 implemented the upgrade system, ubu.8 (d7c) documented it. Feature is fully implemented with version detection, path calculation, systemd resume, error recovery, and documentation."}
-{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-bt5","title":"Implement tailscale installer function in scripts/lib/","description":"## Task

Create `scripts/lib/tailscale.sh` with installation and verification functions.

## Functions to Implement

### install_tailscale()
```bash
install_tailscale() {
    local target_user="${TARGET_USER:-ubuntu}"
    
    # Check if already installed
    if command -v tailscale &>/dev/null; then
        log_detail "Tailscale already installed"
        local version
        version=$(tailscale version | head -1)
        log_detail "  Version: $version"
        return 0
    fi
    
    log_detail "Installing Tailscale..."
    
    # Get Ubuntu codename
    local codename
    codename=$(lsb_release -cs)
    
    # Add Tailscale repository
    curl -fsSL "https://pkgs.tailscale.com/stable/ubuntu/${codename}.noarmor.gpg" \
        | sudo tee /usr/share/keyrings/tailscale-archive-keyring.gpg >/dev/null
    
    echo "deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/tailscale-archive-keyring.gpg] \
        https://pkgs.tailscale.com/stable/ubuntu ${codename} main" \
        | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/tailscale.list
    
    sudo apt-get update
    sudo apt-get install -y tailscale
    
    # Enable and start daemon
    sudo systemctl enable tailscaled
    sudo systemctl start tailscaled
    
    log_success "Tailscale installed"
    log_detail "Run 'sudo tailscale up' to authenticate"
}
```

### verify_tailscale()
```bash
verify_tailscale() {
    if ! command -v tailscale &>/dev/null; then
        log_warn "Tailscale not installed"
        return 1
    fi
    
    local version
    version=$(tailscale version | head -1)
    log_detail "  tailscale: $version"
    
    # Check daemon status
    if systemctl is-active --quiet tailscaled; then
        log_detail "  tailscaled: running"
    else
        log_warn "  tailscaled: not running"
    fi
    
    # Check connection status
    local status
    status=$(tailscale status --json 2>/dev/null | jq -r '.BackendState // "unknown"')
    case "$status" in
        "Running")
            log_detail "  Status: connected"
            local ip
            ip=$(tailscale ip -4 2>/dev/null)
            log_detail "  Tailscale IP: $ip"
            ;;
        "NeedsLogin")
            log_warn "  Status: needs authentication (run 'sudo tailscale up')"
            ;;
        *)
            log_detail "  Status: $status"
            ;;
    esac
    
    return 0
}
```

### check_tailscale_auth()
```bash
# For post-install summary / onboard
check_tailscale_auth() {
    local status
    status=$(tailscale status --json 2>/dev/null | jq -r '.BackendState // "unknown"')
    
    if [[ "$status" == "Running" ]]; then
        return 0  # Authenticated
    else
        return 1  # Needs auth
    fi
}
```

## Integration Points

1. Source from `install.sh` during cli_tools phase
2. Called by `install_cli_tools()` or via manifest-driven execution
3. Verify function used by `acfs doctor`

## Edge Cases

- Ubuntu version without Tailscale repo: Fall back to generic apt repo
- No systemd (containers): Skip service management
- Already authenticated: Don't re-run `tailscale up`","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"PurpleMountain","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:58:01.410847-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T17:31:03.983416-05:00"}

```bash
        return 0
    fi
    
    log_detail "Installing Tailscale..."
    
    # Get Ubuntu codename
    local codename
    codename=$(lsb_release -cs)
    
    # Add Tailscale repository
    curl -fsSL "https://pkgs.tailscale.com/stable/ubuntu/${codename}.noarmor.gpg" \
        | sudo tee /usr/share/keyrings/tailscale-archive-keyring.gpg >/dev/null
    
    echo "deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/tailscale-archive-keyring.gpg] \
        https://pkgs.tailscale.com/stable/ubuntu ${codename} main" \
        | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/tailscale.list
    
    sudo apt-get update
    sudo apt-get install -y tailscale
    
    # Enable and start daemon
    sudo systemctl enable tailscaled
    sudo systemctl start tailscaled
    
    log_success "Tailscale installed"
    log_detail "Run 'sudo tailscale up' to authenticate"
}
```

### verify_tailscale()
```bash
verify_tailscale() {
    if ! command -v tailscale &>/dev/null; then
        log_warn "Tailscale not installed"
        return 1
    fi
    
    local version
    version=$(tailscale version | head -1)
    log_detail "  tailscale: $version"
    
    # Check daemon status
    if systemctl is-active --quiet tailscaled; then
        log_detail "  tailscaled: running"
    else
        log_warn "  tailscaled: not running"
    fi
    
    # Check connection status
    local status
    status=$(tailscale status --json 2>/dev/null | jq -r '.BackendState // "unknown"')
    case "$status" in
        "Running")
            log_detail "  Status: connected"
            local ip
            ip=$(tailscale ip -4 2>/dev/null)
            log_detail "  Tailscale IP: $ip"
            ;;
        "NeedsLogin")
            log_warn "  Status: needs authentication (run 'sudo tailscale up')"
            ;;
        *)
            log_detail "  Status: $status"
            ;;
    esac
    
    return 0
}
```

### check_tailscale_auth()
```bash
# For post-install summary / onboard
check_tailscale_auth() {
    local status
    status=$(tailscale status --json 2>/dev/null | jq -r '.BackendState // "unknown"')
    
    if [[ "$status" == "Running" ]]; then
        return 0  # Authenticated
    else
        return 1  # Needs auth
    fi
}
```

## Integration Points

1. Source from `install.sh` during cli_tools phase
2. Called by `install_cli_tools()` or via manifest-driven execution
3. Verify function used by `acfs doctor`

## Edge Cases

- Ubuntu version without Tailscale repo: Fall back to generic apt repo
- No systemd (containers): Skip service management
- Already authenticated: Don't re-run `tailscale up`","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"PurpleMountain","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:58:01.410847-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T17:34:44.531008-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T17:34:44.531008-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented tailscale.sh with install/verify/auth functions. Added install.sh integration and doctor checks."}
 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-c61","title":"Define session export JSON schema","description":"# Task: Define session export JSON schema

## Context
Part of EPIC: Agent Session Sharing and Replay (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-0sb)

## What to Do
Design the JSON schema for exported agent sessions.

## Schema (inline in export code)

```typescript
// In scripts/lib/session.sh or equivalent
// Schema documented here, not as separate file

interface SessionExport {
    schema_version: 1;
    exported_at: string;  // ISO8601
    session_id: string;
    agent: "claude-code" | "codex" | "gemini";
    model: string;
    summary: string;
    duration_minutes: number;
    stats: {
        turns: number;
        files_created: number;
        files_modified: number;
        commands_run: number;
    };
    outcomes: Array<{
        type: "file_created" | "file_modified" | "command_run";
        path?: string;
        description: string;
    }>;
    key_prompts: string[];
    sanitized_transcript: Array<{
        role: "user" | "assistant";
        content: string;
        timestamp: string;
    }>;
}
```

## Key Design Decisions

1. **Schema lives in code, not separate file**
   - AGENTS.md: "bar for adding files is very high"
   - Schema is simple enough to document inline
   - TypeScript interface provides validation

2. **Version field for evolution**
   - `schema_version: 1` allows future changes
   - Importers can handle version differences

3. **Sanitization-aware**
   - Fields designed for post-sanitization data
   - No raw command outputs (may contain secrets)

4. **Focused on value**
   - `outcomes` shows what happened
   - `key_prompts` shows how (for learning)
   - Not a raw dump of everything

## Validation
```bash
validate_session_export() {
    local file="$1"
    
    # Check required fields exist
    jq -e ".schema_version and .session_id and .agent" "$file" >/dev/null || {
        log_error "Invalid session export: missing required fields"
        return 1
    }
    
    # Check version
    local version=$(jq -r ".schema_version" "$file")
    [[ "$version" == "1" ]] || {
        log_warn "Session version $version may not be fully compatible"
    }
    
    return 0
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Schema defined as TypeScript interface (in code comments)
- [ ] Validation function checks required fields
- [ ] Version field present for future evolution
- [ ] NO separate schema.json file
- [ ] NO separate docs file (documented in bead + code)

## Files to Modify (NOT create)
- scripts/lib/session.sh - add validation and schema comments","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T12:46:36.450112-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T15:40:24.438595-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T15:40:24.438595-05:00","close_reason":"Created scripts/lib/session.sh with: (1) TypeScript interface as detailed code comments, (2) validate_session_export function checking required fields, (3) schema_version field for evolution, (4) helper functions for getting schema version/summary/agent. All acceptance criteria met."}
 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-cfk","title":"Write security best practices documentation page","description":"## Task

Create a documentation page explaining our security recommendations and the Google SSO strategy.

## Location

`apps/web/app/docs/security/page.tsx` or `apps/web/content/security.mdx`

## Content Outline

### 1. Introduction
- Why security matters for vibe coding
- The "one account to rule them all" philosophy
- This guide's scope (account security, not VPS hardening)

### 2. The Google SSO Strategy

#### Why We Recommend It
- Single identity across services
- Fewer passwords = fewer weak points
- Google's security infrastructure protects everything
- Easy recovery through Google

#### How It Works
```
Your Google Account
    ├── Tailscale (VPS access)
    ├── Claude.ai (coding agent)
    ├── ChatGPT/Codex (coding agent)
    ├── Gemini (coding agent - native)
    ├── Vercel (deployment)
    └── Supabase (database)
```

#### The Trade-Off
- Single point of failure: if Google compromised, everything is
- Mitigated by: making Google account extremely secure

### 3. Securing Your Google Account

#### Essential Steps (Do These Now)
1. **Enable 2-Step Verification**
   - Go to: https://myaccount.google.com/signinoptions/two-step-verification
   - Use authenticator app, not SMS
   
2. **Review Recovery Options**
   - Recovery email: set a secondary email
   - Recovery phone: set if comfortable
   
3. **Check Connected Apps**
   - Review: https://myaccount.google.com/permissions
   - Remove any you don't recognize

#### Advanced Steps (Highly Recommended)
1. **Use a Strong, Unique Password**
   - Generated by password manager
   - Never used elsewhere
   
2. **Enable Advanced Protection Program**
   - For high-risk users (developers count!)
   - https://landing.google.com/advancedprotection/
   - Requires security keys

3. **Use Security Keys**
   - Hardware keys (YubiKey, Google Titan)
   - Strongest form of 2FA
   - Required for Advanced Protection

### 4. Services Without Google SSO

Some services don't support Google SSO:
- **GitHub**: Email + password (use strong, unique password)
- **Cloudflare**: Email + password

For these:
- Use your primary Google email address
- Generate strong password with password manager
- Enable their native 2FA

### 5. Password Manager Recommendation

If not already using one:
- **1Password**: Best overall
- **Bitwarden**: Best free option
- **Apple Keychain**: Good if Apple ecosystem

Use it for:
- GitHub password
- Cloudflare password
- Any service without Google SSO

### 6. Quick Security Checklist

```
[ ] Google 2-Step Verification enabled
[ ] Google recovery options set
[ ] Using authenticator app (not SMS)
[ ] GitHub 2FA enabled
[ ] Strong, unique passwords for non-SSO services
[ ] Using a password manager
```

### 7. What If Google Gets Compromised?

Steps to take immediately:
1. Change Google password from a trusted device
2. Revoke all connected app permissions
3. Check for unauthorized logins
4. Re-authenticate all SSO services
5. Enable Advanced Protection Program

## Design Notes

- Use callout boxes for important warnings
- Include screenshots for Google settings
- Make checklist interactive (can check off items)
- Mobile-friendly layout","status":"in_progress","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"PinkCastle","created_at":"2025-12-21T17:01:16.35343-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T17:32:21.410497-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-cfk","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-4ey","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T17:03:25.269753-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}
 {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-coj","title":"Create GitHub Action for daily checksum monitoring","description":"# Task: Create GitHub Action for daily checksum monitoring

## Purpose
Automatically detect when upstream installer scripts change and create PRs to update checksums.

## Critical Addition: Verify Stability Before PR

**Problem:** Upstream scripts might change multiple times in a day during a release.
**Solution:** Verify checksum is stable across 2 checks before creating PR.

## Workflow Design

```yaml
name: Monitor Upstream Checksums
on:
  schedule:
    - cron: "0 6 * * *"   # 6 AM UTC daily
    - cron: "0 18 * * *"  # 6 PM UTC daily (stability check)
  workflow_dispatch:       # Manual trigger

jobs:
  check-checksums:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Fetch current checksums
        id: current
        run: |
          ./scripts/lib/security.sh --verify --json > current.json
          
      - name: Check for changes
        id: changes
        run: |
          # Parse JSON output
          changed=$(jq -r ".changed | length" current.json)
          echo "changed_count=$changed" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          
          if [[ "$changed" -gt 0 ]]; then
            echo "changed=true" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
            jq -r ".changed[].name" current.json > changed_tools.txt
          else
            echo "changed=false" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          fi
          
      - name: Check stability (only if changes detected)
        if: steps.changes.outputs.changed == true
        id: stability
        run: |
          # Load previous run checksums from cache
          if [[ -f ".checksum-cache/previous.json" ]]; then
            prev_checksums=$(jq -r ".checksums" .checksum-cache/previous.json)
            curr_checksums=$(jq -r ".checksums" current.json)
            
            if [[ "$prev_checksums" == "$curr_checksums" ]]; then
              echo "stable=true" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
              echo "Checksums stable across 2 runs - safe to PR"
            else
              echo "stable=false" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
              echo "Checksums changed since last run - waiting for stability"
            fi
          else
            echo "stable=false" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
            echo "First run with changes - caching for stability check"
          fi
          
          # Cache current state
          mkdir -p .checksum-cache
          cp current.json .checksum-cache/previous.json
          
      - name: Update checksums file
        if: steps.changes.outputs.changed == true && steps.stability.outputs.stable == true
        run: |
          ./scripts/lib/security.sh --update-checksums > checksums.yaml
          
      - name: Create Pull Request
        if: steps.changes.outputs.changed == true && steps.stability.outputs.stable == true
        uses: peter-evans/create-pull-request@v5
        with:
          title: "chore(security): update upstream checksums"
          body: |
            ## Upstream Checksum Updates
            
            The following installer checksums have changed:
            $(cat changed_tools.txt | sed "s/^/- /")
            
            ### Stability Verified
            ✅ Checksums stable across 2 consecutive checks (12 hours apart)
            
            ### Action Required
            Please review the upstream changes before merging:
            $(cat changed_tools.txt | while read tool; do
              url=$(jq -r ".installers.$tool.url" checksums.yaml)
              echo "- [$tool]($url)"
            done)
            
            ---
            🤖 Auto-generated by checksum monitoring workflow
          branch: auto/update-checksums
          labels: security, automated
```

## Stability Check Logic

```
Run 1 (6 AM): Detects bun checksum changed
  → Cache new checksum
  → No PR yet

Run 2 (6 PM): Same bun checksum as cached
  → Stable = true
  → Create PR

Run 2 (6 PM): Different bun checksum than cached  
  → Stable = false
  → Update cache
  → Wait for next run
```

## Dependencies
- Depends on: JSON output mode (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-ss3)
- Creates: Auto-generated PRs

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Runs twice daily (12 hours apart)
- [ ] Stability check requires 2 matching runs
- [ ] Only creates PR when checksums are stable
- [ ] PR body lists changed tools with links
- [ ] Manual trigger available for testing","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T12:45:53.040595-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T14:55:17.637256-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T14:55:17.637256-05:00","close_reason":"GitHub Action workflow created for daily checksum monitoring with stability checking","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-coj","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-ss3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T12:47:37.794807-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}
